title: Minimize admission of privileged containers
text: A privileged container can access host devices and kernel capabilities and effectively runs as root on the node, breaking the isolation between the container and the host. Set securityContext.privileged to false and only request privileged mode when there is no alternative.

title: Minimize containers allowing privilege escalation
text: Containers should set allowPrivilegeEscalation to false so a process cannot gain more privileges than its parent process. Allowing escalation lets a compromised process move toward root and weakens the security boundary.

title: Minimize admission of root containers
text: Containers should run as a non-root user by setting runAsNonRoot to true and providing a fixed non-zero runAsUser. Running as root increases the blast radius of a container escape because the attacker inherits root on the node.

title: Minimize containers sharing the host network namespace
text: Setting hostNetwork to true places the pod directly on the node network, bypassing network policies and exposing host interfaces. Avoid hostNetwork unless a workload genuinely needs node-level network access.

title: Minimize containers sharing the host process namespace
text: hostPID lets a container see and interact with all processes on the node, which can be used to read secrets from other processes or interfere with the host. Leave hostPID unset (false) for normal workloads.

title: Minimize containers sharing the host IPC namespace
text: hostIPC gives a container access to the node's inter-process communication, allowing it to read or tamper with shared memory used by other processes. Keep hostIPC disabled unless explicitly required.

title: Drop the NET_RAW capability
text: The NET_RAW capability allows crafting raw packets, which enables spoofing and certain network attacks from inside a pod. Drop NET_RAW (and ideally all capabilities) unless the workload needs low-level network access.

title: Minimize containers with added capabilities
text: Adding Linux capabilities beyond the default set expands what a compromised container can do on the node. Containers should not add capabilities unless each one is justified and documented.

title: Drop all capabilities and add back only what is needed
text: The safest baseline is securityContext.capabilities.drop set to ALL, then adding back only the specific capabilities a workload requires. Starting from a full capability set leaves unnecessary privileges available to an attacker.

title: Apply a seccomp profile
text: A seccomp profile restricts the system calls a container can make, shrinking the kernel attack surface. Set seccompProfile to RuntimeDefault (or a tailored profile) rather than leaving it unconfined.

title: Minimize use of hostPath volumes
text: hostPath volumes mount a directory from the node into the pod, which can expose sensitive host files or allow writing to the host filesystem. Prefer ephemeral or managed volume types and avoid hostPath where possible.

title: Use a read-only root filesystem
text: Setting readOnlyRootFilesystem to true prevents an attacker who gains code execution from writing to the container filesystem, limiting persistence and tampering. Mount writable paths explicitly as separate volumes where the app needs them.

title: Apply a default-deny network policy
text: By default all pods can talk to all other pods. A default-deny NetworkPolicy per namespace, with explicit allow rules, limits how far an attacker can move laterally after compromising one pod.

title: Define network policies for each namespace
text: Each namespace should have NetworkPolicies controlling ingress and egress so traffic between workloads is restricted to what is intended. Without them, a compromised service can reach every other service in the cluster.

title: Minimize use of the cluster-admin role
text: cluster-admin grants unrestricted access to the entire cluster. Bind it to as few subjects as possible and prefer narrowly scoped roles, so a compromised account cannot take over the cluster.

title: Minimize wildcard use in Roles and ClusterRoles
text: Using wildcards (*) for verbs, resources, or apiGroups grants broad, often unintended permissions. Roles should list the specific actions and resources required, following least privilege.

title: Do not use the default service account for workloads
text: The default service account is shared and easy to over-permission. Create dedicated service accounts per workload so permissions can be scoped and audited independently.

title: Mount service account tokens only when required
text: Automatically mounting the service account token into every pod exposes API credentials a workload may not need. Set automountServiceAccountToken to false unless the pod actually calls the Kubernetes API.

title: Prefer mounting secrets as files over environment variables
text: Secrets passed as environment variables are easier to leak through logs, crash dumps, or child processes. Mounting secrets as files reduces accidental exposure and supports rotation.

title: Do not deploy workloads to the default namespace
text: The default namespace makes it hard to apply scoped policies and RBAC. Place workloads in named namespaces so network policies, quotas, and access controls can be applied cleanly.

title: Define a liveness probe
text: A liveness probe lets Kubernetes detect and restart a hung container. Without it, a wedged process can keep receiving traffic while failing, hurting reliability.

title: Define a readiness probe
text: A readiness probe stops traffic being routed to a pod until it can actually serve requests. Missing it causes errors during startup, deploys, and recovery.

title: Set CPU requests
text: CPU requests tell the scheduler how much CPU a pod needs, enabling sensible placement and fair sharing. Without requests, a noisy neighbour can starve other workloads.

title: Set CPU limits
text: CPU limits cap how much CPU a container can consume, preventing a single workload from monopolising a node. Set limits appropriate to the workload to protect cluster stability.

title: Set memory requests
text: Memory requests inform scheduling and protect against overcommitment. Without them the scheduler cannot reason about a pod's memory footprint, risking evictions.

title: Set memory limits
text: Memory limits prevent a container from consuming all node memory and triggering out-of-memory kills of other pods. Define limits so one workload cannot destabilise the node.

title: Pin image tags instead of using latest
text: Using the latest tag (or no tag) means the running image can change unexpectedly between pulls, making deployments non-reproducible and complicating rollback. Reference a specific, immutable tag.

title: Set imagePullPolicy to Always for mutable tags
text: If a tag can change, imagePullPolicy should be Always so nodes do not silently run a stale cached image. With pinned digests this matters less, but mutable tags need a fresh pull.

title: Reference images by digest
text: Pinning an image by its SHA256 digest guarantees the exact bytes that run, defeating tag mutation and tampering. Prefer image@sha256:... over a tag for production workloads.

title: Use a read-only root filesystem where possible
text: A read-only root filesystem blocks an attacker from modifying binaries or dropping tools inside the container. Combine it with explicit writable volumes for any paths the app legitimately writes to.

title: Minimize container capabilities
text: Containers should drop all Linux capabilities and add back only those strictly needed. Excess capabilities give a compromised container more ways to affect the node.

title: Disallow privilege escalation
text: Containers should set allowPrivilegeEscalation to false so a process cannot gain more privileges than its parent. Allowing privilege escalation makes it easier for an attacker to move from a compromised process toward root.

title: Drop all default capabilities
text: Containers should drop ALL Linux capabilities and then add back only the narrow set they actually need. Leaving the default capability set in place gives an attacker more primitives if the container is compromised.

title: Do not leave default capabilities intact
text: A container should not run with the unmodified default Linux capability set. Explicitly dropping capabilities reduces the blast radius of container escape and privilege misuse.

title: Set CPU limits
text: CPU limits cap how much processor time a container can consume, preventing one workload from monopolising node capacity. Define realistic limits to protect cluster stability.

title: Do not run containers as root
text: Containers should run as a non-root user by setting runAsNonRoot to true and using a fixed non-zero runAsUser. Running as root greatly increases the impact of any container escape or application compromise.

title: Avoid latest image tags
text: Using the latest image tag makes deployments non-reproducible and allows image contents to change without review. Reference a specific immutable tag or digest instead.

title: Use a read-only root filesystem
text: A read-only root filesystem makes it harder for an attacker to modify binaries or persist tools inside the container. Mount only the specific writable paths an application needs.

title: Set CPU requests
text: CPU requests tell the scheduler how much processor time a pod needs and help prevent starvation by noisy neighbours. Every workload should declare a sensible baseline request.

title: Set memory requests
text: Memory requests inform placement decisions and reduce the risk of scheduling too many workloads onto one node. Without requests, eviction and instability become more likely under pressure.

title: Do not run privileged containers
text: Privileged containers can access host devices and kernel features and effectively break container isolation. Keep privileged set to false unless there is a tightly justified operational requirement.

title: Set memory limits
text: Memory limits stop one container from exhausting node memory and impacting other workloads. Set limits appropriate to the service so runaway memory growth is contained.

title: Use a non-root UID above 10000
text: Workloads should run with a fixed high, non-zero UID rather than root or low-numbered users. A higher dedicated UID reduces overlap with privileged host accounts and supports least privilege.

title: Use a non-root GID above 10000
text: Containers should run with a dedicated, non-root group ID that is not in the low privileged system range. Using a high GID reduces the chance of inheriting sensitive host or image group semantics.

title: Set RuntimeDefault seccomp profile
text: Containers should set seccompProfile to RuntimeDefault or a tailored local profile to restrict available system calls. Leaving seccomp unset exposes a larger kernel attack surface.

title: Do not disable seccomp enforcement
text: Seccomp should remain enabled so containers are constrained to an expected system-call surface. Disabling seccomp removes a key kernel hardening control.

title: Restrict added capabilities to NET_BIND_SERVICE only when needed
text: If a workload must add Linux capabilities, NET_BIND_SERVICE should usually be the only one allowed for binding to low ports. Additional capabilities expand the actions an attacker can take after compromise.

title: Do not deploy workloads to the default namespace
text: The default namespace makes it harder to apply clean RBAC, quotas, and network policies. Place workloads in dedicated namespaces so security boundaries can be managed explicitly.

title: Define a default container security context
text: Every workload should set an explicit securityContext baseline, including non-root execution and other hardening defaults, so safe settings are not left to image or platform defaults. A missing security context leads to inconsistent and weaker runtime posture.

title: Block public access on S3 buckets
text: S3 buckets should enable the account- and bucket-level public access block settings so objects are not unintentionally exposed to the internet. Public buckets are a frequent source of data leaks.

title: Enable encryption at rest for S3 buckets
text: S3 buckets should have default server-side encryption enabled so stored objects are encrypted at rest. This protects data if the underlying storage is ever exposed.

title: Avoid unrestricted ingress to administrative ports
text: Security groups should not allow 0.0.0.0/0 ingress to sensitive ports such as SSH (22) or RDP (3389). Open admin ports are scanned and brute-forced constantly; restrict to known source ranges.

title: Avoid wildcard actions and resources in IAM policies
text: IAM policies that grant Action: * or Resource: * give far more access than usually intended. Scope policies to the specific actions and ARNs required, following least privilege.

title: Enable encryption for RDS storage
text: RDS instances should have storage encryption enabled so database data is encrypted at rest. Encryption must be set at creation time, so it belongs in the infrastructure definition.

title: Encrypt EBS volumes
text: EBS volumes should be encrypted so data on disk and in snapshots is protected. Enabling account-level default EBS encryption is a simple way to enforce this everywhere.

title: Do not hardcode secrets in infrastructure code
text: API keys, passwords, and tokens should never be committed in Terraform or manifests. Use a secrets manager or injected variables so credentials are not exposed in version control.

title: Run the container as a non-root user
text: A Dockerfile should set a non-root USER so the container does not run as root by default. Running as root amplifies the impact of any container breakout.

title: Pin base image versions
text: Base images should be referenced by a specific version or digest rather than latest, so builds are reproducible and you control when the base changes. Floating tags lead to surprise breakage and drift.

title: Do not bake secrets into images
text: Secrets copied into image layers remain retrievable from the image history even if later deleted. Pass secrets at runtime or via build secrets rather than embedding them in the image.
